title: MongoDB Replication - Complete Guide (Beginner to Advanced)
description: Comprehensive guide on replica sets, replication, failover, and high availability

replication_overview.definition: Replication maintains copies of data across multiple MongoDB servers for high availability and redundancy
replication_overview.concept: Replica set: cluster of MongoDB servers where one is primary (writes) and others are secondaries (read-only copies)
key_components: Primary: Accepts writes, replicates to secondaries
Secondary: Read-only copy, syncs with primary
Arbiter: Votes in elections, doesn't hold data
Oplog: Operation log (sequential record of all changes)
Heartbeat: Periodic communication to detect failures
replication_overview.minimum_setup: 3 nodes (1 primary + 2 secondaries or 1 primary + 1 secondary + 1 arbiter)

definitions.definition: Group of MongoDB instances maintaining same data
Node that accepts all write operations
Node that replicates data from primary
Node that votes but doesn't store data

definitions.use: Secondaries read oplog to stay synchronized

definitions.definition: Periodic message between replica set members
Process to select new primary if current fails
definitions.trigger: Primary unavailable or primary-secondary connection lost

section_2_beginner_setup.title: BEGINNER: Setting Up Replica Sets

beginner_examples.title: Start MongoDB instances as replica set
Check replica set status
beginner_examples.output: Status of all members, their roles, oplog info

important_notes: All instances in replica set use same --replSet name
Secondaries catch up by reading oplog

section_3_beginner_failover.title: BEGINNER: Automatic Failover
section_3_beginner_failover.definition: Automatic selection of new primary when current primary fails
behavior: Remaining members vote for new primary
Application redirects writes to new primary

beginner_examples.title: Primary node fails
beginner_examples.scenario: 3-node replica set: Primary on node 1, Secondaries on nodes 2&3

beginner_examples.title: Primary recovers
beginner_examples.behavior: Rejoins as secondary, catches up on missed operations

section_3_beginner_failover.write_concern: Determines how many replicas must acknowledge write before success

section_4_intermediate_read_preference.title: INTERMEDIATE: Read Preferences
section_4_intermediate_read_preference.definition: Control which replica set member handles read operations

section_5_intermediate_write_concern.title: INTERMEDIATE: Write Concern
section_5_intermediate_write_concern.definition: Specify how many replicas must acknowledge write before declaring success

levels.name: Majority committed
levels.safety: High (data preserved even if primary fails)

intermediate_examples.title: Write with timeout
intermediate_examples.behavior: Returns error if majority not confirmed in 5 seconds

section_6_intermediate_oplog.title: INTERMEDIATE: Oplog (Operation Log)
section_6_intermediate_oplog.definition: Capped collection storing all operations for replication
characteristics: Secondaries read from oplog to replicate

intermediate_examples.title: Check oplog status
intermediate_examples.shows: Oplog size, data in oplog, replication lag

intermediate_examples.title: Monitor replication lag
intermediate_examples.lag: Difference between primary and secondary times

section_6_intermediate_oplog.oplog_exhaustion: Secondary too far behind to recover (oplog rolled over)

section_7_advanced_arbiter.title: ADVANCED: Arbiter Nodes
section_7_advanced_arbiter.definition: Node that votes but doesn't store data
use_cases: Odd number of voting members (3-node: 2 data + 1 arbiter)

advanced_examples.title: Add arbiter to replica set
advanced_examples.result: Node joins as arbiter, doesn't replicate data

section_7_advanced_arbiter.important_note: Arbiter doesn't hold data, useful for cost savings but reduced redundancy

beginner.benefit: If primary fails, secondary takes over
Can restore from secondary without impacting primary
Primary handles writes, secondaries handle analytics

intermediate.benefit: Failover to secondary with minimal downtime

advanced.example: Update one secondary at a time

practice_1_minimum_three_nodes.why: Need majority for elections and failover
practice_1_minimum_three_nodes.setup: 1 primary + 2 secondaries OR 1 primary + 1 secondary + 1 arbiter

practice_2_use_majority_concern.rule: Use w: majority for important data

mistake_1_two_node_setup.problem: If one fails, remaining node can't get majority

mistake_2_ignore_replication_lag.problem: Secondary falls too far behind, can't catch up
mistake_2_ignore_replication_lag.solution: Monitor lag, alert on excessive delay

mistake_3_strong_consistency_on_secondar.mistake: Read from secondary for critical data

scenarios.recovery: Only remaining member can't get majority, stuck (bring back one)
Manual intervention needed (remove one, restart as primary)
Secondary too far behind, must resync from primary

summary.core_concept: Replica set maintains multiple copies with automatic failover
summary.write_path: Write to primary → Primary records in oplog → Secondaries read oplog → Replication complete
summary.read_path: Application reads primary by default, can readPref secondary/nearest
summary.best_practice: 3+ nodes, w: majority for important writes, monitor lag, reliable network
summary.progression: Beginner: Basic concepts and setup → Intermediate: Read/write preferences, monitoring → Advanced: Topology and recovery
